subject: Chemistry
q: n moles of a non-volatile and non-electrolyte solute are dissolved in N moles of a solvent to form an ideal solution. If P is the vapour pressure of the solution and '$P^o$' is the vapour pressure of the pure solvent, then
question: n moles of a non-volatile and non-electrolyte solute are dissolved in N moles of a solvent to form an ideal solution. If P is the vapour pressure of the solution and '$P^o$' is the vapour pressure of the pure solvent, then
solution:
For an ideal solution containing a non-volatile, non-electrolyte solute, only the solvent contributes to the vapour pressure.

According to Raoult’s law,

$$ P = P^o x_{\text{solvent}} $$

Here, moles of solvent $= N$ and moles of solute $= n$.

So the mole fraction of solvent is

$$ x_{\text{solvent}}=\frac{N}{N+n} $$

Hence,

$$ P = P^o \times \frac{N}{N+n} $$

Now,

$$ P^o - P = P^o - P^o\frac{N}{N+n} $$

$$ P^o - P = P^o\left(1-\frac{N}{N+n}\right) $$

$$ P^o - P = P^o\frac{n}{N+n} $$

$$ \frac{P^o-P}{P} = \frac{P^o\frac{n}{N+n}}{P^o\frac{N}{N+n}} $$

$$ \frac{P^o-P}{P}=\frac{n}{N} $$

So the correct option is

$$ \boxed{\frac{P^o-P}{P}=\frac{n}{N}} $$

Correct answer: Option A.

subject: Chemistry
q: An ideal liquid mixture is prepared from two volatile liquids A ($p^\circ = 100$ torr) and B ($p^\circ = 200$ torr). The mixture has 1 mole of A and 4 mole of B. The total vapour pressure of the distillate will be :
question: An ideal liquid mixture is prepared from two volatile liquids A ($p^\circ = 100$ torr) and B ($p^\circ = 200$ torr). The mixture has 1 mole of A and 4 mole of B. The total vapour pressure of the distillate will be :
solution:
For the given liquid mixture,

$$n_A = 1,\qquad n_B = 4$$

So mole fractions in the original liquid mixture are

$$x_A=\frac{1}{1+4}=\frac{1}{5}$$

$$x_B=\frac{4}{5}$$

For an ideal solution, according to Raoult’s law,

$$p_A=x_Ap_A^\circ$$

$$p_B=x_Bp_B^\circ$$

So,

$$p_A=\frac{1}{5}\times 100=20\text{ torr}$$

$$p_B=\frac{4}{5}\times 200=160\text{ torr}$$

Total vapour pressure above the original solution is

$$P=p_A+p_B=20+160=180\text{ torr}$$

But the question asks for the vapour pressure of the distillate.

The distillate is formed by condensing the vapour, so its composition will be the same as the vapour composition.

Vapour phase mole fractions are:

$$y_A=\frac{p_A}{P}=\frac{20}{180}=\frac{1}{9}$$

$$y_B=\frac{p_B}{P}=\frac{160}{180}=\frac{8}{9}$$

Now this distillate acts as a new liquid mixture. Its total vapour pressure will be

$$P' = y_Ap_A^\circ+y_Bp_B^\circ$$

$$P'=\frac{1}{9}\times 100+\frac{8}{9}\times 200$$

$$P'=\frac{100+1600}{9}$$

$$P'=\frac{1700}{9}=188.88\text{ torr}$$

So the correct option is:

$$\boxed{188.88\text{ torr}}$$

Option C.

subject: Chemistry
q: A liquid is placed inside a closed container. A very light glass plate having a small hole is gently put over the liquid surface. The vapour pressure of the liquid inside the container will be:
question: A liquid is placed inside a closed container. A very light glass plate having a small hole is gently put over the liquid surface. The vapour pressure of the liquid inside the container will be:
options: Greater than the value obtained when the glass plate is not present
Equal to the value obtained when the glass plate is not present
Smaller than the value obtained when the glass plate is not present
solution:
The vapour pressure will be the same as it would be if the glass plate were removed.

Vapour pressure of a liquid in a closed vessel depends mainly on:

  • the nature of the liquid

  • the temperature

It does not depend on the exposed surface area of the liquid.

When the glass plate with a small hole is placed on the liquid surface, the exposed area becomes smaller. So evaporation may become slower at first. But since there is still a small hole, vapour can form and fill the space above the liquid.

After some time, equilibrium is reached between evaporation and condensation. At this equilibrium, the vapour pressure becomes the saturated vapour pressure of the liquid at that temperature.

So the glass plate only affects the rate of reaching equilibrium, not the final vapour pressure.

Hence, the correct option is:

$$\boxed{\text{Option B: Same as what would be if the glass plate were removed}}$$

subject: Chemistry
q: The vapour pressure of water is determined by :
question: The vapour pressure of water is determined by :
solution:
The correct option is:

Option C: Temperature

The vapour pressure of water depends mainly on its temperature.

When water is kept in a closed container, some water molecules evaporate and form vapour. After some time, the rate of evaporation becomes equal to the rate of condensation. At this stage, the pressure exerted by the vapour is called vapour pressure.

For a given liquid like water, vapour pressure does not depend on:

  • surface area of the container

  • volume of the container

as long as some liquid water is still present in the container.

If the temperature increases, more water molecules get enough energy to escape into the vapour phase. So, the vapour pressure increases.

Hence, the vapour pressure of water depends upon temperature only.

Answer: Option C.

subject: Chemistry
q: At 50°C, pure benzene, C6H6, has a vapour pressure of 268 Torr. What number of moles of a non-volatile solute must be added per mole of benzene to obtain a benzene solution with vapour pressure 167 Torr at 50°C?
question: At 50°C, pure benzene, C6H6, has a vapour pressure of 268 Torr. What number of moles of a non-volatile solute must be added per mole of benzene to obtain a benzene solution with vapour pressure 167 Torr at 50°C?
solution:
For a solution containing a non-volatile solute, Raoult’s law is used:

$$ p = x_{\text{benzene}} \, p^0 $$

Here,

$$ p^0 = 268 \text{ Torr} $$

$$ p = 167 \text{ Torr} $$

So,

$$ x_{\text{benzene}}=\frac{p}{p^0} $$

$$ x_{\text{benzene}}=\frac{167}{268} $$

Now suppose the solution contains $1$ mol of benzene and $n$ mol of non-volatile solute.

Then mole fraction of benzene is

$$ x_{\text{benzene}}=\frac{1}{1+n} $$

So,

$$ \frac{1}{1+n}=\frac{167}{268} $$

Taking reciprocal,

$$ 1+n=\frac{268}{167} $$

$$ n=\frac{268}{167}-1 $$

$$ n=\frac{101}{167} $$

$$ n=0.605 $$

So, the number of moles of non-volatile solute required per mol of benzene is

$$ \boxed{0.605} $$

Correct option: B.

subject: Chemistry
q: At places situated at high altitude, water starts boiling below $100^{\circ}$C due to
question: At places situated at high altitude, water starts boiling below $100^{\circ}$C due to
options: increase in the amount of heavy water
solution:
The correct answer is Option B: atmospheric pressure is low.

Water boils when its vapour pressure becomes equal to the atmospheric pressure.

At sea level, atmospheric pressure is normal, so water boils at $100^\circ \text{C}$.

At higher altitudes, the atmospheric pressure is lower. So, water does not need to be heated up to $100^\circ \text{C}$ for its vapour pressure to become equal to the outside pressure. It starts boiling at a lower temperature.

So, at higher altitudes, water boils below $100^\circ \text{C}$ because atmospheric pressure is low.

subject: Chemistry
q: At 350 K, $p_A^\circ = 300$ torr and $p_B^\circ = 800$ torr. The mole fraction of the mixture whose normal boiling point is 350 K is :
question: At 350 K, $p_A^\circ = 300$ torr and $p_B^\circ = 800$ torr. The mole fraction of the mixture whose normal boiling point is 350 K is :
solution:
For an ideal liquid mixture, according to Raoult’s law,

$$ p_{\text{total}} = X_A p_A^\circ + X_B p_B^\circ $$

Given:

$$ p_A^\circ = 300 \text{ torr} $$

$$ p_B^\circ = 800 \text{ torr} $$

The mixture has a normal boiling point of $350 \text{ K}$.

At normal boiling point, the total vapour pressure of the liquid becomes equal to atmospheric pressure, that is,

$$ p_{\text{total}} = 760 \text{ torr} $$

Also,

$$ X_A + X_B = 1 $$

So,

$$ X_B = 1 - X_A $$

Using Raoult’s law:

$$ 760 = X_A(300) + (1 - X_A)(800) $$

$$ 760 = 300X_A + 800 - 800X_A $$

$$ 760 = 800 - 500X_A $$

$$ 500X_A = 40 $$

$$ X_A = \frac{40}{500} $$

$$ X_A = 0.08 $$

So, the composition of the mixture is:

$$ X_A = 0.08 $$

Correct option: Option A.

subject: Chemistry
q: Let $P_0$ be the vapour pressure of the pure solvent and $P$ be the vapour pressure of its solution. If $N_1$ and $N_2$ denote the mole fractions of the solvent and the non-volatile solute respectively, the correct relation is :
question: Let $P_0$ be the vapour pressure of the pure solvent and $P$ be the vapour pressure of its solution. If $N_1$ and $N_2$ denote the mole fractions of the solvent and the non-volatile solute respectively, the correct relation is :
solution:
For a solution containing a non-volatile solute, only the solvent contributes to the vapour pressure.

According to Raoult’s law,

$$ P = P_0 \times N_1 $$

where:

$P_0$ = vapour pressure of pure solvent

$P$ = vapour pressure of solution

$N_1$ = mole fraction of solvent

Since $N_2$ is the mole fraction of the non-volatile solute, it does not directly contribute to vapour pressure.

So the correct relation is:

$$ P = P_0 N_1 $$

Correct option: B

subject: Chemistry
q: At the same temperature, the vapour pressure of a solution containing a non-volatile solute B in solvent A is 95% of the vapour pressure of pure solvent A. If the molar mass of the solvent is 0.3 times the molar mass of the solute, find the ratio of the mass of solvent to the mass of solute.
question: At the same temperature, the vapour pressure of a solution containing a non-volatile solute B in solvent A is 95% of the vapour pressure of pure solvent A. If the molar mass of the solvent is 0.3 times the molar mass of the solute, find the ratio of the mass of solvent to the mass of solute.
solution:
For a solution containing a non-volatile solute, Raoult’s law says:

$$ \frac{p}{p^0}=x_A $$

where $p$ is the vapour pressure of the solution, $p^0$ is the vapour pressure of the pure solvent, and $x_A$ is the mole fraction of the solvent.

Given:

$$ p=95\% \text{ of } p^0 $$

So,

$$ \frac{p}{p^0}=0.95 $$

Hence,

$$ x_A=0.95 $$

Since the solution contains only solvent A and solute B,

$$ x_B=1-x_A=1-0.95=0.05 $$

Now,

$$ \frac{x_A}{x_B}=\frac{n_A}{n_B} $$

So,

$$ \frac{n_A}{n_B}=\frac{0.95}{0.05}=19 $$

We need the ratio of weight of solvent to solute:

$$ \frac{w_A}{w_B}=\frac{n_A M_A}{n_B M_B} $$

Given:

$$ M_A=0.3M_B $$

So,

$$ \frac{w_A}{w_B}=19 \times \frac{M_A}{M_B} $$

$$ \frac{w_A}{w_B}=19 \times 0.3=5.7 $$

So, the ratio of weight of solvent to solute is:

$$ \boxed{5.7} $$

Correct option: B

subject: Chemistry
q: An aqueous solution is made by dissolving 2 moles of [Cu(NH3)3Cl]Cl in 3 moles of H2O. Its relative lowering of vapour pressure is 0.50. On adding AgNO3, the solution will produce (assuming that the degree of ionisation of the substance does not change after adding AgNO3)
question: An aqueous solution is made by dissolving 2 moles of [Cu(NH3)3Cl]Cl in 3 moles of H2O. Its relative lowering of vapour pressure is 0.50. On adding AgNO3, the solution will produce (assuming that the degree of ionisation of the substance does not change after adding AgNO3)
solution:
For the solution,

Moles of solute $[Cu(NH_3)_3Cl]Cl = 2$

Moles of water $= 3$

Relative decrease in vapour pressure is given by mole fraction of solute particles:

$$ \frac{p^0-p}{p^0}=\frac{i n}{N+i n} $$

Here,

$n=2$, $N=3$, and relative decrease $=0.50$.

So,

$$ 0.50=\frac{2i}{3+2i} $$

$$ 3+2i=4i $$

$$ 3=2i $$

$$ i=1.5 $$

The compound $[Cu(NH_3)_3Cl]Cl$ behaves like a $1:1$ electrolyte:

$$ [Cu(NH_3)_3Cl]Cl \rightleftharpoons [Cu(NH_3)_3Cl]^+ + Cl^- $$

For a $1:1$ electrolyte,

$$ i=1+\alpha $$

So,

$$ 1.5=1+\alpha $$

$$ \alpha=0.5 $$

Only the chloride ion outside the coordination sphere is ionisable and can give precipitate with $AgNO_3$.

Moles of free $Cl^-$ formed:

$$ =2 \times 0.5=1 $$

So, moles of $AgCl$ formed:

$$ =1 \text{ mol} $$

Correct option: A, $1$ mol $AgCl$

subject: Chemistry
q: When 4.0 g of a non-volatile solute is dissolved in 54.0 g of water, the vapour pressure decreases by $1.0 \%$. Find the molar mass of the non-volatile solute.
question: When 4.0 g of a non-volatile solute is dissolved in 54.0 g of water, the vapour pressure decreases by $1.0 \%$. Find the molar mass of the non-volatile solute.
solution:
For a non-volatile solute, the relative lowering of vapour pressure is equal to the mole fraction of the solute.

So,

$$ \frac{p^\circ - p}{p^\circ} = x_{\text{solute}} $$

The vapour pressure is lowered by $1.0\%$, so

$$ \frac{p^\circ - p}{p^\circ} = \frac{1}{100} = 0.01 $$

Mass of water $= 54.0 \text{ g}$

Molar mass of water $= 18 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$

So, moles of water:

$$ n_{\text{water}} = \frac{54.0}{18} = 3.0 \text{ mol} $$

Let the molar mass of the solute be $M$.

Mass of solute $= 4.0 \text{ g}$

So, moles of solute:

$$ n_{\text{solute}} = \frac{4.0}{M} $$

Now,

$$ x_{\text{solute}} = \frac{n_{\text{solute}}}{n_{\text{solute}} + n_{\text{water}}} $$

$$ 0.01 = \frac{\frac{4.0}{M}}{\frac{4.0}{M} + 3.0} $$

$$ 0.01 = \frac{4.0}{4.0 + 3M} $$

$$ 0.01(4.0 + 3M) = 4.0 $$

$$ 0.04 + 0.03M = 4.0 $$

$$ 0.03M = 3.96 $$

$$ M = \frac{3.96}{0.03} $$

$$ M = 132 \text{ g mol}^{-1} $$

The molar mass of the non-volatile solute is

$$ \boxed{132 \text{ g mol}^{-1}} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: What mass of a non-volatile solute (molecular mass $=40$ ) must be dissolved in 114 g octane so that the vapour pressure becomes $80 \%$ of its original value?
question: What mass of a non-volatile solute (molecular mass $=40$ ) must be dissolved in 114 g octane so that the vapour pressure becomes $80 \%$ of its original value?
solution:
For a non-volatile solute dissolved in a volatile solvent, Raoult’s law says:

$$ \frac{p}{p^0}=x_{\text{solvent}} $$

Here the vapour pressure is reduced to $80\%$ of its original value, so

$$ \frac{p}{p^0}=0.80 $$

Hence,

$$ x_{\text{octane}}=0.80 $$

Molar mass of octane, $C_8H_{18}$, is:

$$ 8(12)+18(1)=114\ \text{g mol}^{-1} $$

Given mass of octane $=114\ \text{g}$, so moles of octane:

$$ n_{\text{octane}}=\frac{114}{114}=1 $$

Let moles of solute be $n$.

Now,

$$ x_{\text{octane}}=\frac{n_{\text{octane}}}{n_{\text{octane}}+n_{\text{solute}}} $$

So,

$$ 0.80=\frac{1}{1+n} $$

$$ 1+n=\frac{1}{0.80}=1.25 $$

$$ n=0.25 $$

Moles of solute $=0.25$.

Molecular mass of solute $=40$, so mass of solute:

$$ m=n \times M $$

$$ m=0.25 \times 40=10\ \text{g} $$

So, the required mass of solute is:

$$ \boxed{10\ \text{g}} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: At temperature $T$, the vapour pressures of the pure liquids A and B, which give an ideal solution, are 300 torr and 800 torr respectively. A vapour mixture containing $A$ and $B$ has mole fraction of $A$ equal to 0.25 and is compressed slowly at the same temperature T. After the vapour condenses completely ($100 \%$ condensation), the vapour pressure of the liquid obtained is found to be ' $P$ '. Find the value of 684 - P.
question: At temperature $T$, the vapour pressures of the pure liquids A and B, which give an ideal solution, are 300 torr and 800 torr respectively. A vapour mixture containing $A$ and $B$ has mole fraction of $A$ equal to 0.25 and is compressed slowly at the same temperature T. After the vapour condenses completely ($100 \%$ condensation), the vapour pressure of the liquid obtained is found to be ' $P$ '. Find the value of 684 - P.
solution:
For an ideal solution, Raoult’s law is used:

$$ P = x_A P_A^0 + x_B P_B^0 $$

Given,

$$ P_A^0 = 300 \text{ torr}, \qquad P_B^0 = 800 \text{ torr} $$

The vapour mixture has mole fraction of $A$ as $0.25$.

On $100\%$ condensation, the whole vapour mixture converts into liquid. So the composition of the liquid condensate will be the same as the original vapour mixture.

Thus,

$$ x_A = 0.25 $$

and

$$ x_B = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75 $$

Now,

$$ P = (0.25)(300) + (0.75)(800) $$

$$ P = 75 + 600 $$

$$ P = 675 \text{ torr} $$

So,

$$ 684 - P = 684 - 675 = 9 $$

Hence, the value of $684 - P$ is

$$ \boxed{9} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: How much mass of a solute (molecular mass 60) must be dissolved in 180 g of water so that the vapour pressure becomes $4 / 5^{\text {th }}$ of that of pure water?
question: How much mass of a solute (molecular mass 60) must be dissolved in 180 g of water so that the vapour pressure becomes $4 / 5^{\text {th }}$ of that of pure water?
solution:
For a non-volatile solute dissolved in water, Raoult’s law says:

$$ \frac{p}{p^0}=x_{\text{water}} $$

Here, the vapour pressure is reduced to $\frac{4}{5}$ of pure water, so

$$ \frac{p}{p^0}=\frac{4}{5} $$

Thus,

$$ x_{\text{water}}=\frac{4}{5} $$

Mass of water $=180 \text{ g}$

Molar mass of water $=18 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$

So, moles of water:

$$ n_{\text{water}}=\frac{180}{18}=10 $$

Let the moles of solute be $n$.

Mole fraction of water is:

$$ x_{\text{water}}=\frac{n_{\text{water}}}{n_{\text{water}}+n_{\text{solute}}} $$

$$ \frac{10}{10+n}=\frac{4}{5} $$

$$ 50=40+4n $$

$$ 4n=10 $$

$$ n=2.5 $$

So, moles of solute required $=2.5$.

Molecular weight of solute $=60$

Mass of solute:

$$ w=n \times M $$

$$ w=2.5 \times 60=150 \text{ g} $$

So, the required weight of solute is:

$$ \boxed{150 \text{ g}} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: A solution made by adding 6 g of a non-volatile solute to 180 g of water has a vapour pressure of 20 torr. When 1 mole of water is added further to this solution, its vapour pressure rises by 0.02 torr. The molar mass (in $\mathrm{g} / \mathrm{mol}$ ) of the non-volatile solute is
question: A solution made by adding 6 g of a non-volatile solute to 180 g of water has a vapour pressure of 20 torr. When 1 mole of water is added further to this solution, its vapour pressure rises by 0.02 torr. The molar mass (in $\mathrm{g} / \mathrm{mol}$ ) of the non-volatile solute is
solution:
For a solution containing a non-volatile solute, Raoult’s law is used:

$$ p = p^\circ x_{\text{water}} $$

where $p^\circ$ is the vapour pressure of pure water and $x_{\text{water}}$ is the mole fraction of water.

Mass of water initially $=180\,\text{g}$

$$ \text{Moles of water}=\frac{180}{18}=10 $$

Let the molar mass of solute be $M$.

Mass of solute $=6\,\text{g}$

$$ \text{Moles of solute}=\frac{6}{M} $$

Initial vapour pressure is $20$ torr, so

$$ 20=p^\circ \times \frac{10}{10+\frac{6}{M}} $$

After adding $1$ mole of water, moles of water become $11$.

New vapour pressure is

$$ 20+0.02=20.02 \text{ torr} $$

So,

$$ 20.02=p^\circ \times \frac{11}{11+\frac{6}{M}} $$

Now divide the second equation by the first:

$$ \frac{20.02}{20} = \frac{\frac{11}{11+\frac{6}{M}}}{\frac{10}{10+\frac{6}{M}}} $$

$$ 1.001 = \frac{11\left(10+\frac{6}{M}\right)}{10\left(11+\frac{6}{M}\right)} $$

Let

$$ \frac{6}{M}=n $$

Then,

$$ 1.001=\frac{11(10+n)}{10(11+n)} $$

$$ 1.001 \times 10(11+n)=11(10+n) $$

$$ 10.01(11+n)=110+11n $$

$$ 110.11+10.01n=110+11n $$

$$ 0.11=0.99n $$

$$ n=\frac{0.11}{0.99}=\frac{1}{9} $$

So,

$$ \frac{6}{M}=\frac{1}{9} $$

$$ M=54 $$

The molar mass of the non-volatile solute is

$$ \boxed{54\ \text{g mol}^{-1}} $$
